fallback.text:
TypeScript tip: 'satisfies' is the operator I reach for the most these days.

It lets you check that an object matches a type WITHOUT widening or narrowing it.

Get the inference, keep the safety. Cleanest pattern in modern TS.

cards.relevanceReason: Modern TypeScript patterns worth knowing while you code

fallback.text:
Claude 4.7 is now available. The 1M context window means you can drop entire codebases in a single prompt and get coherent edits across the whole thing.

We rebuilt our internal eval harness on it last week. Cuts our turnaround time in half.

cards.relevanceReason: Latest from the Claude team — relevant to your agent work

fallback.text:
An underused trick: use clip-path: inset() to animate reveals.

You can clip from any side, animate the inset values, and get hardware-accelerated reveals without overflow:hidden hacks.

Works beautifully for hold-to-delete buttons and image reveals.

cards.relevanceReason: Animation techniques that elevate any UI

fallback.text:
Your monolith is fine.

Most of the 'we need microservices' talk is engineers wanting to play with new toys, not actual scaling problems.

A single Postgres can handle the load of nearly every startup. Boring is a feature.

The biggest UX shift in coding agents: people are starting to *trust* multi-step plans.

2024: 'show me a diff'
2026: 'go fix these 14 things, ping me when done'

The agent loop is the new IDE.

cards.relevanceReason: Industry shift in how devs use AI agents

fallback.text:
Hot take: most useEffect bugs are state-shape bugs.

If your effect has 5 dependencies and a guard clause, your state is wrong. Restructure the state until the effect collapses to one dep or disappears entirely.

cards.relevanceReason: React patterns to keep in your head

fallback.text:
Build in public means you write down what you tried, what failed, what worked. Six months later you'll have a corpus that the next version of you (and every other dev with the same problem) can search.

The blog post IS the product moat.

INP > LCP for 2026.

INP measures actual interaction latency — every click, every tap. Optimizing it forces you to fix the long tasks that LCP-only optimization missed.

If you only watch one Web Vital next year, watch this one.

pnpm 10 is genuinely faster than npm AND uses less disk because of content-addressable storage.

Every project I touch I switch the lockfile. Hasn't bitten me once.

cards.relevanceReason: You're using pnpm in this project — confirms the choice

fallback.text:
Copy-paste components > library imports.

You own the code, you customize without forking, you upgrade by re-copying. Every framework will get to this eventually — shadcn just got there first.

Postgres learned a lot of new tricks in 17:

- JSON_TABLE for parsing JSON into rows natively
- Incremental backups
- Way better vacuum on huge tables

You probably don't need that NoSQL store.

Webhook security checklist:

1. Verify signature (HMAC of the raw body)
2. Check timestamp (reject > 5 min old)
3. Idempotency key on your side
4. Rate limit per source
5. Log everything

Get 1-3 wrong and you have a replay attack vulnerability.

cards.relevanceReason: Security patterns relevant when wiring integrations
